data.title: Apple Set to Launch iPhone Fold with Innovative Design in 2026
data.description:
Apple is reportedly preparing to launch its first foldable smartphone, iPhone Fold, in the fall of 2026. Industry analysts and multiple supply chain sources have confirmed that the company is in the production preparation phase. The iPhone Fold will feature a book-style foldable design similar to the Samsung Galaxy Z Fold, with a thickness of 9-9.5mm when folded and approximately 4.5-4.8mm when unfolded.

The device's frame will be constructed from titanium alloy to prevent bending, and the hinge will be crafted from a combination of titanium alloy and stainless steel, ensuring both durability and smooth folding.

This stock alert was generated using automated technology and GuruFocus financial data to provide readers with timely and accurate market reporting. data.title: Microsoft Options Market Signals Limited Hedging Despite 17% Slide
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT, Financials) has fallen more than 17% this year, but options market activity suggests institutional investors are not rushing to hedge against further losses.

The analysis of the volatility skew for options on March 20 reveals that the implied volatility around the current share price stays very much the same. Put volatility goes up a little bit at lower strike prices, but the growth seems to be gradual rather than an indication of aggressive downside protection.

The structure shows normal hedging activities instead of a sudden need for insurance. On the plus side, call options don't exhibit any extraordinary surges in implied volatility, which supports the idea that traders expect prices to stay stable.

The Black-Scholes-based estimates show that Microsoft's implied one-standard-deviation range through March 20 is between $376.94 and $423.83. That means that markets expect considerable volatility even if they have been weak lately.

Wall Street analysts still have a mostly favorable view. The company has a Strong Buy consensus rating and an average 12-month price target of about $593, which means it might go up a lot from where it is now.

Investors will keep an eye on earnings reports, Azure's expansion in AI, and the overall mood in the IT industry to see if the current drop stabilizes or gets worse in the next several weeks.

data.title: Alphabet Unveils Gemini 3.1 Pro AI Model
data.description:
Alphabet (GOOG, Financials) introduced Gemini 3.1 Pro, the latest update to its flagship artificial intelligence model, highlighting improvements in reasoning and complex problem-solving.

Google said Gemini 3.1 Pro achieved a verified score of 77.1% on the ARC-AGI-2 benchmark, which measures a model's ability to solve new logic patterns. The company said that result more than doubled the reasoning performance of Gemini 3 Pro.

The new version is available to developers through the Gemini API in Google AI Studio, Gemini CLI and Android Studio. It is also accessible to enterprise customers via Vertex AI and Gemini Enterprise, and to consumers through the Gemini app and NotebookLM.

Alphabet previously said Gemini surpassed 740 million monthly active users, reflecting broad adoption across consumer and enterprise platforms.

Shares of Alphabet were little changed in late morning trading following the announcement.

Investors will watch whether continued model upgrades translate into stronger cloud revenue, higher enterprise adoption and competitive gains in the rapidly evolving AI market.

data.title: AMD to Backstop $300M Crusoe Loan With AI Chips
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD, Financials) is preparing to backstop a $300 million loan to data center operator Crusoe to support deployment of its artificial intelligence accelerators, according to a report.

The loan, provided by Goldman Sachs, will be backed by AMD's AI chips and related equipment, the report said, citing people familiar with the matter. The arrangement is intended to finance installation of AMD hardware at a data center in Ohio.

As part of the agreement, AMD has agreed to rent the chips from Crusoe if the company cannot find sufficient third-party customers to use the equipment. The backing reportedly helped Crusoe secure a 6% interest rate, lower than it might otherwise have obtained.

The structure resembles financing models used to accelerate AI infrastructure buildouts as chipmakers compete for market share in data centers. By supporting customer financing, AMD may increase adoption of its Instinct accelerators in a market dominated by rival offerings.

AMD shares rose modestly in midday trading following the report. Investors will monitor potential financial exposure, demand trends and margin impact as AI infrastructure spending continues to expand.

data.title: Amazon Remains Morgan Stanley's Top Pick on GenAI Momentum
data.description:
Amazon.com (AMZN, Financials) remains Morgan Stanley's top stock pick, with analysts arguing the company's AI positioning in both cloud and retail is not fully reflected in its valuation.

The firm maintains an Overweight rating and a $300 price target. Shares rose about 2% trading. Morgan Stanley estimates Amazon is trading at roughly 19 times its 2027 GAAP earnings per share forecast, implying about 20% forward EPS growth. That represents what analysts describe as a significant discount to large-cap peers on a PEG basis.

According to the research note led by Brian Nowak, Amazon Web Services and the company's retail platform are both positioned as GenAI winners. Analysts expect AWS revenue growth to exceed 30%, supported by a strong backlog that signals durable enterprise demand. However, they noted that AWS growth remains partly constrained by data center capacity, making capital expenditures a key lever for acceleration.

The second major catalyst is what Morgan Stanley calls Agentic Commerce. The firm expects Amazon to expand horizontal AI partnerships while strengthening vertical leadership through its AI-powered shopping assistant, Rufus. Analysts believe this could reinforce Amazon's ecosystem advantage as generative AI reshapes online shopping behavior.

Morgan Stanley said clearer evidence of sustained AWS acceleration and visible returns on AI-related capital spending could help re-rate the stock and rebuild investor confidence in return on invested capital.

With AWS scaling infrastructure and retail integrating AI-driven tools, the firm views Amazon as one of the most underappreciated beneficiaries of the generative AI cycle heading into 2027.

data.title: Alphabet Teams Up With Sea on AI for Shopee, Garena
data.description:
Alphabet (GOOGL, Financials) said its Google unit has formed a strategic partnership with Sea Ltd to develop artificial intelligence tools for Sea’s e-commerce and gaming businesses.

Under the agreement, the companies will explore building an AI “agentic shopping” prototype for Sea’s Shopee platform, according to a joint statement. The initiative aims to expand AI capabilities beyond basic queries to more complex shopping and workflow tasks.

Shopee was Southeast Asia’s largest e-commerce platform in 2024 with a 52% market share, according to consultancy Momentum Works. The new AI features could strengthen its position as competition intensifies, including from Alibaba’s Lazada platform.

Google and Sea’s gaming arm Garena will also deploy AI solutions to improve productivity in game development. The companies said the tools are intended to streamline development processes and enhance user engagement.

The partnership follows a 2024 collaboration between Shopee and YouTube in the region’s e-commerce market.

data.title:
Michael Burry Sounds Alarm on Palantir -- Warns Shares Could Fall to $46
        (Status: Pending)
data.description:
Palantir Technologies (PLTR, Financial) is again under pressure after investor Michael Burry (Trades, Portfolio) released a detailed report suggesting the stock may be overvalued. Burry said Palantir's recent gains might not last. He outlined a scenario where the stock could fall to around $46, down sharply from recent levels, following a 26% drop in the past few weeks.

The investor questioned how Palantir allocates labor costs during software deployments, arguing it inflates profit margins and makes the company look more profitable than it is. He also critiqued leadership and corporate culture, suggesting market enthusiasm is tied more to story than fundamentals.

Wall Street analysts disagreed. D.A. Davidson's Gil Luria said the report doesn't change his outlook, highlighting Palantir's rapid growth and a 137% jump in U.S. commercial sales.

Palantir expects 2026 revenue of $7.19 billion, up about 60% from last year, with strong cash reserves and low debt providing a buffer against market swings.

Technical analysts flagged chart patterns hinting at possible further downside, with initial support levels lower than current trading prices around $131.

data.title: Nvidia Sells Final Arm Stake, Unloads 1.1 Million Shares
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA, Financial) has moved to formally close a chapter that once reshaped the semiconductor landscape. A recent regulatory filing shows the company sold 1.1 million shares of Arm Holdings (ARM, Financial) in the fourth quarter of last year, valued at roughly $140 million based on Arm's Tuesday closing price, reducing its stake to zero. Nvidia declined to comment, and Arm did not immediately respond, but the transaction effectively severs the last remaining equity link between the two companies following a deal that unraveled several years ago.

Investors will remember that Nvidia agreed in 2020 to acquire Arm for $40 billion in what was expected to become the largest transaction in chip industry history. The proposal quickly drew scrutiny from regulators and customers, many of whom viewed Arm's independence as critical given that its technology underpins most of the world's advanced semiconductors. In February 2022, the companies terminated the agreement, and Arm, majority-owned by SoftBank Group (SOBKY, Financial), proceeded with plans to sell shares to the public, reshaping its path as a standalone entity.

The exit also comes as Nvidia, now the world's most valuable company, continues to deploy capital across the broader technology ecosystem. The company holds stakes in Intel (INTC), Nokia (NOK, Financial), CoreWeave (CRWV, Financial) and Synopsys (SNPS, Financial), and has indicated it intends to use its cash to accelerate the adoption of artificial intelligence computing. Against that backdrop, divesting the final Arm position could simplify Nvidia's investment portfolio and potentially sharpen its focus on AI-driven opportunities, a theme that remains central to its long-term capital allocation strategy.

data.title: Japan Launches Up to $36 Billion US Energy Investment Under $550 Billion Pact
data.description:
Japan's long-trailed $550 billion investment framework with the US is now starting to translate into tangible projects, with up to $36 billion earmarked for oil, gas and critical mineral developments in the first tranche of funding. President Donald Trump characterized the launch as the start of a “MASSIVE Trade Deal,” linking the investment drive to the 15% tariff rate agreed on Japanese products, including reduced duties on automobiles. Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi framed the initiative as a way to reinforce economic security and resilient supply chains across energy, artificial intelligence and critical minerals, suggesting Tokyo is positioning capital toward areas viewed as strategically important while still seeking stable returns.

The anchor project is a natural gas facility in Ohio expected to generate 9.2 gigawatts of power. According to the Commerce Department fact sheet, Japan could invest up to $33 billion in the plant, which will be led by a subsidiary of SoftBank Group Corp., with Toshiba Corp. and Hitachi Ltd. expressing interest in participating. If the facility operates at full capacity, it would be equivalent to nine nuclear reactors or roughly the power consumption of about 7.4 million homes on the PJM Interconnection grid. The timing could be notable, as demand from new data centers tied to the artificial intelligence buildout has increased pressure on US energy supply planning. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said the structure is designed so Japan provides the capital, the infrastructure is built in the US, and proceeds are arranged so Japan earns its return while the US expands strategic industrial capacity.

Additional projects include a $2.1 billion investment in the Texas GulfLink deepwater crude export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico, to be operated by Sentinel Midstream, which the Commerce Department said could generate as much as $30 billion in annual US crude exports at full capacity. Japan is also expected to invest $600 million in a high-pressure, high-temperature synthetic industrial diamond grit facility in Georgia involving Element Six, a subsidiary of De Beers, producing materials used in semiconductor, automotive and energy manufacturing. Financing is expected to involve the Japan Bank for International Cooperation and Nippon Export and Investment Insurance, with most of the broader $550 billion mechanism structured around loans and guarantees rather than direct cash investment. With Takaichi scheduled to meet Trump in Washington on March 19, implementation of these projects could become a focal point as both sides work to move from political commitments to operational execution.
